result:
The rain came down in that specific way it only does in late October, not quite a storm but something heavier than a drizzle, the kind that makes every surface slick and reflective like the city has been varnished overnight. I stood under the awning of the closed florist on 14th Street watching water race along the gutters carrying cigarette butts and lost leaves toward the drains. There’s a strange comfort in watching things being carried away. It reminds you that entropy isn’t just a concept in physics textbooks; it’s the quiet engine of every Tuesday afternoon.

I’ve been thinking a lot about disappearance lately. Not the dramatic kind, no vanishing acts or witness protection, but the slow, ordinary kind. The way your favorite sweater gradually becomes a rag you use to clean motorcycle parts. The way songs you once knew by heart now only return in fragments when you’re half-asleep. The way people you loved become stories you tell newer people who never met them, and with each retelling the story gets slightly more polished, slightly less true.

My grandmother used to say that memory is like bread: it goes stale if you don’t consume it quickly enough. She died when I was nineteen and I’ve been trying to eat those memories before they mold for fourteen years now. Some nights I can still taste her specific chicken soup, the one with the tiny meatballs and the dill that stung your sinuses. Other nights I can’t remember what her laugh sounded like, only that it existed. The absence itself has become a kind of presence.

There’s a Japanese concept called *mono no aware* – the gentle sadness at the transience of things. The cherry blossoms fall precisely because they’re beautiful, and their beauty is tied to how briefly they exist. I think about this every time I see someone taking a video of fireworks instead of just watching them. We’ve become a species that tries to possess beauty by capturing it, and in doing so we often miss the very thing we’re trying to keep.

Last week I watched a man in Central Park try to photograph a falcon that had landed on a lamppost. He had one of those enormous lenses that costs more than my first car, and he was so focused on getting the perfect shot that he never once looked at the bird with his naked eyes. The falcon stayed for maybe three minutes, then launched itself into the air with that casual violence that only raptors seem to possess. The man kept adjusting his camera for another thirty seconds before realizing his subject had vanished. I wanted to tell him that the falcon had given him something better than a photograph – it had given him a moment of pure, useless wonder. But I didn’t. Some lessons only hurt when someone else tries to teach them.

I’ve started collecting abandoned books. Not in the organized way serious collectors do, with first editions and protective slipcases. I mean the ones I find on subway seats, in laundromat windowsills, behind park benches. Someone’s discarded paperback that still carries the ghost of their thumbs on the pages. There’s something profoundly intimate about reading a book that someone else gave up on. You can see where they stopped caring – the creased page, the coffee stain, the sudden cessation of marginal notes. One book I found had angry underlined passages in the first third, then nothing. Whatever had enraged the previous reader had either been resolved or had swallowed them whole. I’ll never know which.

The city at 3 a.m. has its own language. The subway vents breathe warm air that smells of iron and forgotten dreams. Delivery bikes slice through the streets with their illegal headlights cutting tunnels in the darkness. There are moments when you can stand on a corner and feel the entire weight of eight million sleeping people pressing against the edges of your consciousness. It’s not loneliness exactly. It’s more like being alone in a cathedral built entirely of other people’s stories.

I tried meditation again recently. The app told me to observe my thoughts without judgment. This is apparently very good for you. My thoughts immediately began performing. *Look at me,* they said. *I am a very interesting thought about mortality. Please observe me in great detail.* The more I tried to watch them drift by like clouds, the more they turned into a ticker-tape parade of every embarrassing thing I’ve ever done since approximately 1997. The brain, it turns out, is a contrary bastard that doesn’t like being watched.

There’s a saxophonist who plays near the farmers’ market on Saturdays. He’s there in all weather, even when it’s so cold his fingers must be numb. He plays old standards with a kind of weary tenderness, like he’s apologizing to the music for what time has done to it. I’ve never given him money. Instead I just stand there for ten minutes every week and let the sound move through me like weather. I think he understands this arrangement. Some transactions don’t need currency.

My neighbor across the hall is slowly going blind. He told me this while we were both waiting for the elevator, as casually as if he were mentioning he’d run out of milk. “The world is getting smaller,” he said, “but also much more interesting.” He’s started memorizing the building by sound – the particular squeak of the third step on the staircase, the way the radiator in 4B clanks at 6:15 every morning like it’s clearing its throat. I find myself trying to notice these things too, trying to learn the secret language of spaces before I need it.

Sometimes I wonder if that’s what growing older really is: the gradual transformation of the world from something you look at to something you listen to, smell, feel in your bones. The visual fireworks give way to deeper resonances. The bright surfaces peel back and you’re left with the grain and texture of things. The way light moves across a wooden table that’s been in your family for three generations. The particular way your mother says your name when she’s worried about you. These things don’t photograph well but they endure.

The rain has stopped now. The streetlights are painting perfect reflections on the wet pavement, turning the sidewalk into a second, inverted city. A woman walks past with a small dog wearing rain boots. The dog looks deeply embarrassed by this but also secretly pleased at being cared for so absurdly. I understand that dog completely.

I think that’s what I’m trying to say, in the end. We’re all just trying to care for each other in increasingly absurd ways while the rain keeps falling and the falcons keep leaving and the books keep getting abandoned on subway seats. The trick isn’t to stop the disappearing. That’s impossible. The trick is to pay such close attention to what’s here that when it leaves, it leaves behind an imprint shaped exactly like love.

The saxophonist will be back on Saturday. I’ll stand in the cold and let the music move through me again. The blind man will continue mapping his shrinking world by sound. The falcon may or may not return to its lamppost. The books will keep finding new abandoned homes.

And I will keep walking through this varnished city, collecting moments like loose change, knowing they’ll eventually spill through holes in my pockets but being grateful for the weight of them while they last.

(Word count: 1,012)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
